summary: Jeff Atwood expresses frustration that Microsoft hasn't updated Windows Script Host to support .NET languages, leaving developers stuck with aging VBScript for quick scripting tasks. While third-party solutions exist, none carry official Microsoft support and each has compatibility issues. He references a 2001 Microsoft post promising WSH.NET plans, noting that three years later nothing has materialized. The post highlights the gap between Microsoft's .NET-centric direction and the practical needs of developers who rely on lightweight scripting.
key_points: WSH with VBScript remains useful for quick utilities that don't warrant full .NET console applications
VBScript's similarity to VB.NET creates confusion as developers accidentally try newer syntax
Third-party WSH.NET alternatives exist but lack official blessing, compatibility, and require separate installation
Microsoft promised WSH.NET plans in 2001 but delivered nothing in the following three years
The disconnect between .NET's dominance and WSH's stagnation creates friction for practical scripting workflows
money_quotes: I still use WSH to write quick and dirty command line utilities that don't justify a full blown .NET console executable.
Like UNIX shell scripts, there's a lot of power there for relatively little effort.
VBScript – the shared script language of classic ASP and the Windows Scripting Host – is rapidly being left behind in an increasingly .NET-centric world.
The worst thing is, it's just similar enough to fool you into trying some VB.NET syntax, even though you know better.
This isn't the same as a Microsoft blessing.
Whatever those 'plans' were... 3 years later, we're still waiting.
key_insight: Microsoft's failure to modernize Windows Script Host with .NET support left a frustrating gap between their strategic platform direction and the practical scripting needs of everyday developers.
